rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 3, PLAINTIFF MARTHE SCHREIBER'S Motion For Monetary And Evidentiary Sanctions For Pursuant To C.C.P. Sections 128, 177.5, 2023.010, 2023.030, 2023.040 And The Courts Inherent Authority To Punish Contempt. The reasoning and recommendation of the judge pro tem is adopted with a modification. The expert witnesses are required to comply with the subpoenas and the order dated December 28, 2015 by producing the requested document directly to Ms. Redamonti with the required affidavits and plaintiff must pay one-half of the costs for that production. Monetary sanctions are denied. rulings.Case Title: AZAR AFSHARIPOUR VS. SAKS FIFTH AVENUE, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Plaintiff Azar Afsharipour For Attorneys Fees And Costs Pursuant To Government Code 12965(B)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 5, PLAINTIFF AZAR AFSHARIPOUR'S Motion Of For Attorneys Fees And Costs Pursuant To Government Code 12965(B). Plaintiff's motion for attorney's fees is granted in part. Plaintiff is awarded $607,601 in attorney's fees, including her counsel's work on this motion and the motion to tax costs. Plaintiff's proffered lodestar of $844,215 is far too high for a relatively simple single plaintiff employment harassment case that was settled a month and half before trial and did not involve any particularly novel or difficult legal issues or an excessive number of motions and the only difficult factual issue was plaintiff's lack of economic damages. Moreover, while there were 20 depositions, many of them were not lengthy, and there was minimal expert work. Based on all the papers filed on this motion, a review of the court file and the court's knowledge of the Bay Area legal marketplace and its experience in handling cases like this one, using plaintiff's proffered lodestar as the starting point, the court has constructed a reasonable lodestar. The reasonable lodestar has been enhanced by a 1.25 multiplier due to the excellent result achieved and the contingent risk. The reasonable lodestar amount of $460,481 was arrived at by: 1) starting with plaintiff's adjusted lodestar on page 11 of her reply brief of $844,215; 2) subtracting $45,515 attributable to the work on the moving papers for this motion; 3) subtracting $9548 attributable to administrative work which is non-compensable overhead; 4) subtracting $48,641 due to the determination that the reasonable rate for Mr. Organ and Mr. Schwartz is $500 per hour; 5) subtracting $164,910 due to the determination that the reasonable rate for the associates, law clerks and paralegals was 40% less than the rates ascribed to them; 6) subtracting $115,120 due to the determination that the hours billed were 20% more than reasonable for this case, there was no need for two primary or lead attorneys and there was considerable duplication of effort. The 1.25 multiplier brings that amount to $575,601. Based on the determination that a reasonable amount for the work done on this motion and the costs motion is $32,000, calculated at 80 hours at a blended rate of $400 per hour, the total amount of fees awarded is $607,601. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: AZAR AFSHARIPOUR VS. SAKS FIFTH AVENUE, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Tax Costs
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 5, DEFENDANT SAKS & COMPANY'S Motion To Tax Costs. Defendant's motion to tax costs is granted in part. Plaintiff is entitled to costs in the amount of $20,140.06. The following claimed costs totaling $20,326.92 are taxed: 1) $217.20 in filing fees for which plaintiff provided no receipts; 2) $6016.92 in deposition costs for which she provided no receipts and $134.81 in food costs; 3) $679.66 in service of process costs for which she provided no receipts; 4) $79.08 in witness fees for which she did not show the reason for their incurrence; 5) $149.08 for a court-ordered transcript since the transcript was not ordered by the court; 6) $2268.41 for models, blowups and photocopying costs since plaintiff has not shown that these costs were incurred for trial exhibits; and 7) $10,559.92 for other costs because those costs were not reasonably necessary for the case, not allowable per CCP 1033.5 and/or no receipts were provided. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

Line, CROSS DEFENDANT LINDSEY SCULLY"S Motion To Compel Production Of Documents; Pro Tem Judge Rebecca Woodson, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rebecca.woodson@dentons.comwith a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/JPT

rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Kathy Arsenault And Parcel Pending, Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Luxer Corporation For Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set One Special Interrogatories, Set One And Request For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Sancfions In The Amount Of $3,505 Against Plaintiff Luxer And Their Counsel
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 8, DEFENDANTS KATHY ARSENAULT AND PARCEL PENDING, INC.'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff Luxer Corporation For Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set One Special Interrogatories, Set One And Request For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Sancfions In The Amount Of $3,505 Against Plaintiff Luxer And Their Counsel. Defendants' motion to compel is granted and their request for sanctions is denied. Subject to a protective order to be issued by the court covering confidential information, plaintiff shall provide full code-compliant responses to all discovery requests at issue in this motion within twenty days of the filing of the order on this motion or within five days of the filing of the protective order, whichever occurs later. rulings.Case Title: VICTOR TONG VS. VINCENT LEUNG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 9, PLAINTIFF VICTOR TONG'S MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. The motion will be heard on 4/1/2016, at 2:00 pm in Department 302 by Judge Quinn. Tong's Motion to Strike Tin's Answer is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. The motion to strike the 23rd affirmative defense is granted because CCP section 411.35 deals with architects, not dental professionals. The motion to strike the 25th and 26th answers is denied because those defenses do not seek affirmative relief. Plaintiff's motion to strike the 1st-3rd, 5th-9th, 11th-13th, 15th, 16th and 20th affirmative defenses on the grounds of uncertainty or insufficient facts is denied because defenses can be stricken only if irrelevant, false, improper, or in order to remove any part of the pleading not drawn or filed in conformity with the laws of the state, a court rule, or an order of the court. (CCP section 436.) The 1st, 6th, 13th, 16th, 21st, and 22nd affirmative defenses cannot be stricken based on Tong's claims that they are false. Striking them on such grounds would require the court to resolve disputes of fact (Tong's allegations vs. Tin's denials), which is improper at this stage; in any event, there exists no evidentiary basis that would allow the court to resolve any factual disputes regarding the truth or falsity of facts relied upon by Tin. Tin shall submit a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: VICTOR TONG VS. VINCENT LEUNG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Answer
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 9, PLAINTIFF VICTOR TONG'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Answer. This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. The motion will be heard on 4/1/2016, at 2:00 pm in Department 302 by Judge Quinn. Tong's Motion to Strike Leung's Answer is DENIED AS MOOT IN PART AND DENIED ON THE MERITS IN PART. The motion to strike Defendant's 21st affirmative defense is rendered moot in light of the ruling on the concurrently-decided demurrer. The 1st, 2nd, 4th, 5th, 14th, 15th, 17th-19th affirmative defenses cannot be stricken based on Tong's claims that they are false. Striking them on such grounds would require the court to resolve disputes of fact (Tong's allegations vs. Leung's denials), which is improper at this stage; in any event, there exists no evidentiary basis that would allow the court to resolve any factual disputes regarding the truth or falsity of facts relied upon by Leung. The 3rd, 4th, 6th, 12th-16th, 17th, and 20th affirmative defenses may depend on facts beyond those raised by Tong, but they are not requests for affirmative relief and, therefore, Tong's motion on such grounds must fail. (See City of Stockton v. Superior Court (2007) 42 Cal.4th 730, 745-746 f. 12.) Leung shall submit a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 9, PLAINTIFF VICTOR TONG'S DEMURRER TO ANSWER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. The motion will be heard on 4/1/2016, at 2:00 pm in Department 302 by Judge Quinn. The court notes that there is no proof of compliance with CCP section 430.41. The parties are to meet and confer in advance of the hearing. If a hearing on the demurrer occurs, Tong will be expected to offer the testimony required under CCP section 430.41(a)(3) at the hearing,. Further failures to comply with CCP section 430.41 will not be excused so readily. Tong's Demurrer to Defendant Leung's Answer is SUSTAINED WITH FIFTEEN DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND IN PART AND OVERRULED IN PART. The demurrer to the 21st answer is sustained with leave to amend because as currently pled it is ambiguous and does not identify a specific portion of the Affordable Care Act. The demurrer to the remaining affirmative defenses is overruled because the affirmative defenses are factually sufficient to constitute defenses. Leung shall submit a proposed order consistent with this tentative. rulings.Case Title: VICTOR TONG VS. VINCENT LEUNG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 9, PLAINTIFF VICTOR TONG'S MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. The motion will be heard on 4/1/2016, at 2:00 pm in Department 302 by Judge Quinn. Tong's Motion to Strike Redwood Dental's Answer is DENIED. The motion to strike Defendant's answers on the grounds that the answers do not allege sufficient facts or are ambiguous must be denied because these are not proper grounds for a motion to strike. A motion to strike can be granted in order to strike irrelevant, false, or improper matter inserted in the pleading or in order to remove any part of the pleading not drawn or filed in conformity with the laws of the state, a court rule, or an order of the court. (CCP section 436.) On this record, Plaintiff's claims that the 1st, 2nd, 4th, 5th, 14th, 15th, 17th-19th affirmative defenses are false would require the court to resolve disputes of fact (Tong's allegations vs. Redwood Dental's denials), which is improper at this stage; in any event, there exists no evidentiary basis that would allow the court to resolve any factual disputes regarding the truth or falsity of facts relied upon by Redwood Dental. The 3rd, 4th, 6th-8th, 12th-16th, 17th, and 20th affirmative defenses may depend on facts beyond those raised by Tong, but they are not requests for affirmative relief and, therefore, Tong's motion on such grounds must fail. (See City of Stockton v. Superior Court (2007) 42 Cal.4th 730, 745-746 f. 12.) Redwood Dental shall submit a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: BAUER'S INTELLIGENT TRANSPORTATION, INC., VS. FADI JOSEPH STEPHAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, April 1, 2016, Line 10, Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction. This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. The motion will be heard on 4/1/2016, at 2:00 pm in Department 302 by Judge Quinn. This matter is on calendar for an order to show cause regarding issuance of a preliminary injunction. Plaintiff Bauer's Intelligent Transportation, Inc.'s request for a preliminary injunction is DENIED. Plaintiff seeks an injunction precluding Defendant Professional Charter Services, LLC ("PCS") from performing any work for any of Bauer's clients who were referred to Defendant PCS by Defendant Fadi Joseph Stephan while Defendant Stephan was employed by Bauer's and which referral Bauer's did not expressly authorize and approve. Stephan last worked for Bauer's in January 2016. Bauer's may be able to establish a right to relief that includes an injunction restraining PCS from engaging in some commercial conduct. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 526, subd. (a)(1).) The verified complaint alleges that Stephan breached duties owed to Bauer's, that PCS was involved in the breaches and that Bauer's established economic relationships were harmed as a result. Stephan filed no opposition refuting the verified allegations and PCS's opposition does not refute these allegations. The verified allegations, if proven, could ground a claim for disruption of economic relationships and justify injunctive relief under the UCL. But, importantly, a permanent injunction along the lines of the temporary relief requested here seems unlikely because it would punish the consumer who did nothing wrong. Further, the other statutory factors, including the balance of hardships, weigh against the requested interim relief. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 526, subds. (a)(1)-(7).) Bauer's has made no substantial showing that PCS's servicing of such clients during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable harm. Indeed, it seems apparent that any breach of duty by Stephan or PCS could be addressed through pecuniary compensation that would not be extremely difficult to ascertain. Nor has Bauer's shown that PCS's servicing of such clients during the litigation will tend to render any judgment ineffectual. Bauer's also seeks an injunction precluding Stephan from making disparaging comments about Bauer's business or its products or services to any of Bauer's existing or potential clients. The court finds that Bauer's has not carried its burden to demonstrate that such a prior restraint on Stephan's speech is justified. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 526.) PCS shall submit a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)
